trans-unit.source: Data Analysis with QGIS
trans-unit.target: Data Analysis with QGIS

trans-unit.source: 🚧This training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧
trans-unit.target: 🚧This training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧

trans-unit.source: __Competences__:
trans-unit.target: __Competences__:

trans-unit.source: This module covers how to perform general data analysis, calculate statistics, create buffers, produce heatmaps, and divide rivers, roads, or areas into segments. The following modules will cover more complicated data analysis methods.
trans-unit.target: This module covers how to perform general data analysis, calculate statistics, create buffers, produce heatmaps, and divide rivers, roads, or areas into segments. The following modules will cover more complicated data analysis methods.

trans-unit.source: Data & Spatial Analysis
trans-unit.target: Data & Spatial Analysis

trans-unit.source: Even with a single layer, extensive analysis is possible. However, often the information we need to analyse is spread across multiple layers. To gain these insights, we use the spatial and non-spatial GIS processing tools we learned in previous modules. In this module, we will explore how to apply these tools, collect and work with data, and create meaningful insights. We will also review several examples of data analysis commonly used in humanitarian work.
trans-unit.target: Even with a single layer, extensive analysis is possible. However, often the information we need to analyse is spread across multiple layers. To gain these insights, we use the spatial and non-spatial GIS processing tools we learned in previous modules. In this module, we will explore how to apply these tools, collect and work with data, and create meaningful insights. We will also review several examples of data analysis commonly used in humanitarian work.

trans-unit.source: Spatial analysis
trans-unit.target: Spatial analysis

trans-unit.source: A spatial analysis can be a result of combining several layers with different information in a single map.
trans-unit.target: A spatial analysis can be a result of combining several layers with different information in a single map.

trans-unit.source: __Geographic analysis helps us answer questions like__:
trans-unit.target: __Geographic analysis helps us answer questions like__:

trans-unit.source: What __patterns__ are in the data?
trans-unit.target: What __patterns__ are in the data?

trans-unit.source: How can we __summarise__ any trends?
trans-unit.target: How can we __summarise__ any trends?

trans-unit.source: What's __nearby__?
trans-unit.target: What's __nearby__?

trans-unit.source: Which areas are __affected__?
trans-unit.target: Which areas are __affected__?

trans-unit.source: What's __inside or outside__ a boundary?
trans-unit.target: What's __inside or outside__ a boundary?

trans-unit.source: How do phenomenon __change with location__?
trans-unit.target: How do phenomenon __change with location__?

trans-unit.source: How do locations __change over time__?
trans-unit.target: How do locations __change over time__?

trans-unit.source: Before doing any sort of processing, you need to __familiarise yourself with the data__ and understand it.
trans-unit.target: Before doing any sort of processing, you need to __familiarise yourself with the data__ and understand it.

trans-unit.source: The first step is to read the metadata from the source and understand __what data was collected__, __who collected the data__, __and how the data was collected__.
trans-unit.target: The first step is to read the metadata from the source and understand __what data was collected__, __who collected the data__, __and how the data was collected__.

trans-unit.source: Next, open the attribute table and look at the different features and attributes available. What do the attributes show and what are they called?
trans-unit.target: Next, open the attribute table and look at the different features and attributes available. What do the attributes show and what are they called?

trans-unit.source: Now you can start visualising the data:
trans-unit.target: Now you can start visualising the data:

trans-unit.source: You can visualise the data cartographically by assigning or categorising the data using symbols
trans-unit.target: You can visualise the data cartographically by assigning or categorising the data using symbols

trans-unit.source: You can create charts from the attribute table
trans-unit.target: You can create charts from the attribute table

trans-unit.source: You can look for patterns, averages, outliers
trans-unit.target: You can look for patterns, averages, outliers

trans-unit.source: We are often seeking ways to __describe__ our data to an audience. Sometimes, spatial analysis will be used to provide recommendations for activities. Given the vast amount of data available online, it is important to take a step back and gain perspective on this knowledge, these capabilities, and the data itself before rushing to manipulate it:
trans-unit.target: We are often seeking ways to __describe__ our data to an audience. Sometimes, spatial analysis will be used to provide recommendations for activities. Given the vast amount of data available online, it is important to take a step back and gain perspective on this knowledge, these capabilities, and the data itself before rushing to manipulate it:

trans-unit.source: __Reliability__: Can I trust this data?
trans-unit.target: __Reliability__: Can I trust this data?

trans-unit.source: __Interest__: Do I need this data?
trans-unit.target: __Interest__: Do I need this data?

trans-unit.source: __Usage__: Am I able to use this data?
trans-unit.target: __Usage__: Am I able to use this data?

trans-unit.source: __Comprehensiveness__: Is this data complete?
trans-unit.target: __Comprehensiveness__: Is this data complete?

trans-unit.source: __Date__: How old is this data?
trans-unit.target: __Date__: How old is this data?

trans-unit.source: __Sensitivity__: Is this data sensitive?
trans-unit.target: __Sensitivity__: Is this data sensitive?

trans-unit.source: With spatial analysis, you can build predictive models to plan ahead of disasters. __HOWEVER: Not all analysis is complicated! Just knowing how many features are in a layer is useful.__ Simple data analysis includes:
trans-unit.target: With spatial analysis, you can build predictive models to plan ahead of disasters. __HOWEVER: Not all analysis is complicated! Just knowing how many features are in a layer is useful.__ Simple data analysis includes:

trans-unit.source: Ranking
trans-unit.target: Ranking

trans-unit.source: Categorising
trans-unit.target: Categorising

trans-unit.source: Above/below threshold
trans-unit.target: Above/below threshold

trans-unit.source: Affected Areas
trans-unit.target: Affected Areas

trans-unit.source: Population distribution
trans-unit.target: Population distribution

trans-unit.source: It is important to know the __limitations__ of the data at your disposal - don't try to use unsuitable data for analysis (e.g. if you now a survey sample is not representative)
trans-unit.target: It is important to know the __limitations__ of the data at your disposal - don't try to use unsuitable data for analysis (e.g. if you now a survey sample is not representative)

trans-unit.source: __There are two main types of data analysis__:
trans-unit.target: __There are two main types of data analysis__:

trans-unit.source: __Thematic analyses__ focus on visual variation according to a given attribute of the data (one of its characteristics). They are performed on a specific field of the attribute table for the layer, whether textual or numerical. The graphical representation (symbology) changes according to the attribute.
trans-unit.target: __Thematic analyses__ focus on visual variation according to a given attribute of the data (one of its characteristics). They are performed on a specific field of the attribute table for the layer, whether textual or numerical. The graphical representation (symbology) changes according to the attribute.

trans-unit.source: For instance: variations in size depending on population numbers in a refugee camp area.
trans-unit.target: For instance: variations in size depending on population numbers in a refugee camp area.

trans-unit.source: __Spatial analyses__ are performed on spatialised phenomena such as: presence/absence of the phenomenon, its relationship with other phenomena or entities, distribution in space. They are performed on the geometry and position of elements, as well as on their relationship with other elements. Spatial analyses can create new values or elements.
trans-unit.target: __Spatial analyses__ are performed on spatialised phenomena such as: presence/absence of the phenomenon, its relationship with other phenomena or entities, distribution in space. They are performed on the geometry and position of elements, as well as on their relationship with other elements. Spatial analyses can create new values or elements.

trans-unit.source: For example: crossing two satellite images to extract flooded areas between two dates; or crossing latrine and water catchment areas in a refugee camp; using a digital elevation model to determine which buildings have a high flooding risk.
trans-unit.target: For example: crossing two satellite images to extract flooded areas between two dates; or crossing latrine and water catchment areas in a refugee camp; using a digital elevation model to determine which buildings have a high flooding risk.

trans-unit.source: Length, Surface, Circumference
trans-unit.target: Length, Surface, Circumference

trans-unit.source: Simply knowing the size of an area or the length of a road section is valuable information. For example, you can determine how much of a road network is inaccessible or how much area is affected by flooding.
trans-unit.target: Simply knowing the size of an area or the length of a road section is valuable information. For example, you can determine how much of a road network is inaccessible or how much area is affected by flooding.

trans-unit.source: These geometrical attributes can be calculated using the [__field calculator__](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_5/en_qgis_non_spatial_tools.html#calculate-field) or the processing tool __"Add geometry attributes"__.
trans-unit.target: These geometrical attributes can be calculated using the [__field calculator__](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_5/en_qgis_non_spatial_tools.html#calculate-field) or the processing tool __"Add geometry attributes"__.

trans-unit.source: The field calculators has the following functions to calculate geometry attributes as new fields in the attribute table:
trans-unit.target: The field calculators has the following functions to calculate geometry attributes as new fields in the attribute table:

trans-unit.source: *Function*
trans-unit.target: *Function*

trans-unit.source: *Description*
trans-unit.target: *Description*

trans-unit.source: Returns the area of the current feature. The area calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and area unit settings.
trans-unit.target: Returns the area of the current feature. The area calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and area unit settings.

trans-unit.source: Returns the length of a linestring. If you need the length of a border of a polygon, use $perimeter instead. The length calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and distance unit settings.
trans-unit.target: Returns the length of a linestring. If you need the length of a border of a polygon, use $perimeter instead. The length calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and distance unit settings.

trans-unit.source: Returns the perimeter length of the current feature. The perimeter calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and distance unit settings.
trans-unit.target: Returns the perimeter length of the current feature. The perimeter calculated by this function respects both the current project's ellipsoid setting and distance unit settings.

trans-unit.source: For example, to calculate the area of polygons:
trans-unit.target: For example, to calculate the area of polygons:

trans-unit.source: Open the attribute table
trans-unit.target: Open the attribute table

trans-unit.source: Open the field calculator
trans-unit.target: Open the field calculator

trans-unit.source: Check the box `Create a new field`
trans-unit.target: Check the box `Create a new field`

trans-unit.source: Enter a `Output field name`: "Area"
trans-unit.target: Enter a `Output field name`: "Area"

trans-unit.source: Select the `Output field type`: In this case we want a number with decimals. So we select either "decimal number".
trans-unit.target: Select the `Output field type`: In this case we want a number with decimals. So we select either "decimal number".

trans-unit.source: Enter `$Area` into the expression window.
trans-unit.target: Enter `$Area` into the expression window.

trans-unit.source: Select `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Select `OK`.

trans-unit.source: In the attribute table, you will find a new column called `Area` with the respective area for each feature.
trans-unit.target: In the attribute table, you will find a new column called `Area` with the respective area for each feature.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Example: Calculating the length of roads
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Example: Calculating the length of roads

trans-unit.source: Basic statistics
trans-unit.target: Basic statistics

trans-unit.source: In the field calculator, we can calculate the length, area, perimeter for each feature of a dataset. However, we might want to have __aggregate statistics__ on a dataset (average length/area, total length/area). QGIS comes with two basic processing tools to generate statistics:
trans-unit.target: In the field calculator, we can calculate the length, area, perimeter for each feature of a dataset. However, we might want to have __aggregate statistics__ on a dataset (average length/area, total length/area). QGIS comes with two basic processing tools to generate statistics:

trans-unit.source: *Processing tool*
trans-unit.target: *Processing tool*

trans-unit.source: *Description*
trans-unit.target: *Description*

trans-unit.source: "__Basic statistics for fields__"
trans-unit.target: "__Basic statistics for fields__"

trans-unit.source: This algorithm generates basic statistics (count, sum, mean, median, standard deviation, quartiles, ...) from the analysis of a values in a field in the attribute table of a vector layer. Numeric, date, time and string fields are supported. The statistics returned will depend on the field type. Statistics are generated as an HTML file.
trans-unit.target: This algorithm generates basic statistics (count, sum, mean, median, standard deviation, quartiles, ...) from the analysis of a values in a field in the attribute table of a vector layer. Numeric, date, time and string fields are supported. The statistics returned will depend on the field type. Statistics are generated as an HTML file.

trans-unit.source: "__Statistics by categories__"
trans-unit.target: "__Statistics by categories__"

trans-unit.source: This algorithm calculates statistics of fields depending on a parent class. In the option `Field to calculate statistics on`, you must select the column that you wish to create statistics for (for example, area/length). In the option `Field(s) with categories` you select the values that will be used as categories in the statistics (for example, flooding=Y/N, type of road, type of building/amenity).
trans-unit.target: This algorithm calculates statistics of fields depending on a parent class. In the option `Field to calculate statistics on`, you must select the column that you wish to create statistics for (for example, area/length). In the option `Field(s) with categories` you select the values that will be used as categories in the statistics (for example, flooding=Y/N, type of road, type of building/amenity).

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Example: Statistics by categories In this example we have a road network which has been intersected with a flood extent. A new field ("Flood") has been calculated containing information whether the road is flooded or not (Y=flooded, N=not flooded). The length of each road has been calculated using the `$length` function in the field calculator as new column called "Length". We want to calculate the total length of flooded and unflooded road respectively.
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Example: Statistics by categories In this example we have a road network which has been intersected with a flood extent. A new field ("Flood") has been calculated containing information whether the road is flooded or not (Y=flooded, N=not flooded). The length of each road has been calculated using the `$length` function in the field calculator as new column called "Length". We want to calculate the total length of flooded and unflooded road respectively.

trans-unit.source: Open the "Statistics by category"-tool
trans-unit.target: Open the "Statistics by category"-tool

trans-unit.source: Select the road network layer.
trans-unit.target: Select the road network layer.

trans-unit.source: Under `Field to calculate statistics on`, select `Length`
trans-unit.target: Under `Field to calculate statistics on`, select `Length`

trans-unit.source: Under `Field(s) with categories`, select `Flood`
trans-unit.target: Under `Field(s) with categories`, select `Flood`

trans-unit.source: Specify a location to save the statistics file.
trans-unit.target: Specify a location to save the statistics file.

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`

trans-unit.source: After completion, a new layer will appear in your layer tab. This will not contain spatial attributes and is a simple attribute table with the statistics. In our case, we will have the basic statistics (min, max, range, sum, median, sd, etc.) for all the road features with the flooding value "Y", and "N".
trans-unit.target: After completion, a new layer will appear in your layer tab. This will not contain spatial attributes and is a simple attribute table with the statistics. In our case, we will have the basic statistics (min, max, range, sum, median, sd, etc.) for all the road features with the flooding value "Y", and "N".

trans-unit.source: Insert statistics examples
trans-unit.target: Insert statistics examples

trans-unit.source: Buffer analysis
trans-unit.target: Buffer analysis

trans-unit.source: Creating a [buffer](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_geoprocessing_wiki.md#buffer) is a helpful analysis to determine __what lies in proximity__ of, for example, a contaminated water source or other hazards and determine vulnerability. Buffer analysis is often used to map the riparian zones along rivers to devise environmental protection zones or estimate vulnerability.
trans-unit.target: Creating a [buffer](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_geoprocessing_wiki.md#buffer) is a helpful analysis to determine __what lies in proximity__ of, for example, a contaminated water source or other hazards and determine vulnerability. Buffer analysis is often used to map the riparian zones along rivers to devise environmental protection zones or estimate vulnerability.

trans-unit.source: Proximity analysis
trans-unit.target: Proximity analysis

trans-unit.source: Estimated vulnerability analysis
trans-unit.target: Estimated vulnerability analysis

trans-unit.source: Density Map Analysis
trans-unit.target: Density Map Analysis

trans-unit.source: Density maps are very useful in communicating the __intensity of a phenomenon in an area__. Point data is __spatially aggregated__ to show the amount of incidents in that area. __For example__, number of schools or number of disease cases.
trans-unit.target: Density maps are very useful in communicating the __intensity of a phenomenon in an area__. Point data is __spatially aggregated__ to show the amount of incidents in that area. __For example__, number of schools or number of disease cases.

trans-unit.source: It is important to consider that most demographic or economic data needs to be __normalised__ (e.g. number of inhabitants). To assess the significance of the number of schools, you will need to know how the population of the area; so the amount of schools per 1,000 inhabitants, or the number of disease cases per 100 persons, for example.
trans-unit.target: It is important to consider that most demographic or economic data needs to be __normalised__ (e.g. number of inhabitants). To assess the significance of the number of schools, you will need to know how the population of the area; so the amount of schools per 1,000 inhabitants, or the number of disease cases per 100 persons, for example.

trans-unit.source: There are a few different types of density maps. The most common are heatmaps and hexagon grid maps. In both cases, the intensity of a phenomenon is calculated with point data (rarely with lines or polygons).
trans-unit.target: There are a few different types of density maps. The most common are heatmaps and hexagon grid maps. In both cases, the intensity of a phenomenon is calculated with point data (rarely with lines or polygons).

trans-unit.source: Heatmaps
trans-unit.target: Heatmaps

trans-unit.source: Heat maps use features in a dataset to calculate the __relative density of points__ on a map. The density is displayed as a colour ramp with colours ranging from "cool" (low density) to "hot" (high density). Heatmaps are useful when you have a large number of features covering an area with areas __where these features cluster together__ and help us visualise __spatial patterns__ of a layer.
trans-unit.target: Heat maps use features in a dataset to calculate the __relative density of points__ on a map. The density is displayed as a colour ramp with colours ranging from "cool" (low density) to "hot" (high density). Heatmaps are useful when you have a large number of features covering an area with areas __where these features cluster together__ and help us visualise __spatial patterns__ of a layer.

trans-unit.source: To create a heatmap you first need a layer containing data points or 'samples'. These points are distributed in an area with some areas containing more than others. The __density__ of the points in space determines the intensity of the colour on the heat map.
trans-unit.target: To create a heatmap you first need a layer containing data points or 'samples'. These points are distributed in an area with some areas containing more than others. The __density__ of the points in space determines the intensity of the colour on the heat map.

trans-unit.source: In QGIS, there are two methods to create heatmaps. The first method uses the symbology tab and is generally a lot faster. The second method uses the interpolation tool __"Heatmap (Kernel Density Estimation)"__ and offers more parameters to adjust. The advantage of the processing tool is that you can set a radius using metric units (for example the number of points in a __100 Meters__ radius compared to using the millimetres or pixels of your computer screen) and set a variable radius that is determined by another attribute. The next section discusses the creation of heatmaps using the symbology tab.
trans-unit.target: In QGIS, there are two methods to create heatmaps. The first method uses the symbology tab and is generally a lot faster. The second method uses the interpolation tool __"Heatmap (Kernel Density Estimation)"__ and offers more parameters to adjust. The advantage of the processing tool is that you can set a radius using metric units (for example the number of points in a __100 Meters__ radius compared to using the millimetres or pixels of your computer screen) and set a variable radius that is determined by another attribute. The next section discusses the creation of heatmaps using the symbology tab.

trans-unit.source: insert link
trans-unit.target: insert link

trans-unit.source: Using the symbology tab to create a heatmap
trans-unit.target: Using the symbology tab to create a heatmap

trans-unit.source: You can create a heatmap in the __symbology tab__ of a point or polyline layer. Navigate to the symbology tab and select the `Heatmap` symbolisation method. Here, you can adjust the __colour ramp, radius, and maximum value__. The __radius__ (in millimetres on your screen) determines the size of the circle that is used to aggregate the points. If it gets bigger more points can be aggregated and the 'heat' increases. The __maximum value__ determines the value that is given the 'hottest' colour. By default, it is set to the highest number of aggregated points. For example, you can __set a threshold__ above which everything has the "hottest" colour. Reducing it changes the visualisation drastically.
trans-unit.target: You can create a heatmap in the __symbology tab__ of a point or polyline layer. Navigate to the symbology tab and select the `Heatmap` symbolisation method. Here, you can adjust the __colour ramp, radius, and maximum value__. The __radius__ (in millimetres on your screen) determines the size of the circle that is used to aggregate the points. If it gets bigger more points can be aggregated and the 'heat' increases. The __maximum value__ determines the value that is given the 'hottest' colour. By default, it is set to the highest number of aggregated points. For example, you can __set a threshold__ above which everything has the "hottest" colour. Reducing it changes the visualisation drastically.

trans-unit.source: As you can see, the information communicated through the different maps changes drastically. This is why you need to be transparent on what parameters you have set to create the heatmap.
trans-unit.target: As you can see, the information communicated through the different maps changes drastically. This is why you need to be transparent on what parameters you have set to create the heatmap.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Assigning a weight to the samples
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Assigning a weight to the samples

trans-unit.source: Assigning weight to sample points can be useful when your dataset has additional information (such as the type of incident, or sampled amount of rainfall) and you want to integrate this information into your heatmap.
trans-unit.target: Assigning weight to sample points can be useful when your dataset has additional information (such as the type of incident, or sampled amount of rainfall) and you want to integrate this information into your heatmap.

trans-unit.source: Hex Maps (Hexagon Grids)
trans-unit.target: Hex Maps (Hexagon Grids)

trans-unit.source: Hexagon grids are used to aggregate point incidents in order to normalise geographic data or to mitigate the modifiable area unit problem (problems arising from using irregular shaped polygons). In GIS, we commonly use rectangles (e.g. raster data) or __hexagons__, as these geometries can be repeat in an evenly spaced grid without leaving gaps.
trans-unit.target: Hexagon grids are used to aggregate point incidents in order to normalise geographic data or to mitigate the modifiable area unit problem (problems arising from using irregular shaped polygons). In GIS, we commonly use rectangles (e.g. raster data) or __hexagons__, as these geometries can be repeat in an evenly spaced grid without leaving gaps.

trans-unit.source: The advantage of using hexagons is that it is a polygon that closely resembles a __circle__ (where the distance to the centre is equal at every point along the outline), but still __leaves no gaps__ when placed as a __grid__. This means that it is also possible to use absolute values (no normalisation), since the spatial units have the same size.
trans-unit.target: The advantage of using hexagons is that it is a polygon that closely resembles a __circle__ (where the distance to the centre is equal at every point along the outline), but still __leaves no gaps__ when placed as a __grid__. This means that it is also possible to use absolute values (no normalisation), since the spatial units have the same size.

trans-unit.source: Another advantage is that you can use the hexagon grid as spatial units and combine multiple variables (for example, number of incidents per population size) or calculate indexes.
trans-unit.target: Another advantage is that you can use the hexagon grid as spatial units and combine multiple variables (for example, number of incidents per population size) or calculate indexes.

trans-unit.source: WIKI: __Hexagon grids__ are especially useful for density maps. For example, the number of conflict events or water points in an area.
trans-unit.target: WIKI: __Hexagon grids__ are especially useful for density maps. For example, the number of conflict events or water points in an area.

trans-unit.source: To create a hexagon grid map, you will first need to [create a hexagon grid](link), by using the "__Create Grid__" vector tool.
trans-unit.target: To create a hexagon grid map, you will first need to [create a hexagon grid](link), by using the "__Create Grid__" vector tool.

trans-unit.source: Next, you will need to join the point data with the hexagon grid. We want to know the amount of points that are inside of a hexagon cell. To count the number of points, we need to use the vector tool "__Count points in polygon__". The result will be a hexagonal grid where each polygon has the a value for the number of points in that area.
trans-unit.target: Next, you will need to join the point data with the hexagon grid. We want to know the amount of points that are inside of a hexagon cell. To count the number of points, we need to use the vector tool "__Count points in polygon__". The result will be a hexagonal grid where each polygon has the a value for the number of points in that area.

trans-unit.source: The final step will be to __visualise__ the data by assigning a __graduated symbology__ to the polygons. You can play around with the transparency of your layers to make more information visible.
trans-unit.target: The final step will be to __visualise__ the data by assigning a __graduated symbology__ to the polygons. You can play around with the transparency of your layers to make more information visible.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Example video: Creating a hex map
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Example video: Creating a hex map

trans-unit.source: Create a Hexagon grid with the tool "__Create Grid__".
trans-unit.target: Create a Hexagon grid with the tool "__Create Grid__".

trans-unit.source: Select `Hexagon (Polygon)` as __Grid type__.
trans-unit.target: Select `Hexagon (Polygon)` as __Grid type__.

trans-unit.source: The Grid extent should be set to the layer/area of interest.
trans-unit.target: The Grid extent should be set to the layer/area of interest.

trans-unit.source: Select the horizontal and vertical spacing according to the scale of your map.
trans-unit.target: Select the horizontal and vertical spacing according to the scale of your map.

trans-unit.source: Optional: Remove the unnecessary Polygons.
trans-unit.target: Optional: Remove the unnecessary Polygons.

trans-unit.source: Use the tool "__Count points in Polygon__" to add an attribute field with the number of points that are *inside* each hexagon cell. The __Polygons__ field should be your reference layer. The new layer will have an attribute field called "__NUMPOINTS__"
trans-unit.target: Use the tool "__Count points in Polygon__" to add an attribute field with the number of points that are *inside* each hexagon cell. The __Polygons__ field should be your reference layer. The new layer will have an attribute field called "__NUMPOINTS__"

trans-unit.source: Assign a __graduated__ symbology to the `Count`-layer. Select "__NUMPOINTS__" as the value and categorise the classes as you wish.
trans-unit.target: Assign a __graduated__ symbology to the `Count`-layer. Select "__NUMPOINTS__" as the value and categorise the classes as you wish.

trans-unit.source: Analysis by joining attributes
trans-unit.target: Analysis by joining attributes

trans-unit.source: [Joining](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_non_spatial_joins_wiki.md) datasets is a common and useful way to get new insights by adding the information of one table to the other, taking into account key attributes that are used to identify the features that are to be joined. For example: the population size of a district is in one table, and the number of hospitals in a district is in a second table and you wish to combine the two tables to know how many hospitals per population size are in the respective districts.
trans-unit.target: [Joining](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_non_spatial_joins_wiki.md) datasets is a common and useful way to get new insights by adding the information of one table to the other, taking into account key attributes that are used to identify the features that are to be joined. For example: the population size of a district is in one table, and the number of hospitals in a district is in a second table and you wish to combine the two tables to know how many hospitals per population size are in the respective districts.

trans-unit.source: You have two separate data tables with information you wish to aggregate (join);
trans-unit.target: You have two separate data tables with information you wish to aggregate (join);

trans-unit.source: Both tables share key identifiers; `CNTY_NAME` in this example
trans-unit.target: Both tables share key identifiers; `CNTY_NAME` in this example

trans-unit.source: The key identifiers serve as the __relationship__ between the two tables
trans-unit.target: The key identifiers serve as the __relationship__ between the two tables

trans-unit.source: The tables will be combined via the key identifiers
trans-unit.target: The tables will be combined via the key identifiers

trans-unit.source: Joining tables will create a new table where the attribute values are added to the key identifiers
trans-unit.target: Joining tables will create a new table where the attribute values are added to the key identifiers

trans-unit.source: Pivoting tables
trans-unit.target: Pivoting tables

trans-unit.source: Sometimes, the tables are not in a suitable format for joining. For example, having multiple zones per country makes the CNTRY_NAME field unsuitable for aggregation. In such cases, pivoting the table is useful. This involves aggregating the fields for the zones and their respective area sizes under the country. The values in the ZONE column will be transformed into columns containing the area values. This way, you can aggregate this table with additional information that includes data on countries.
trans-unit.target: Sometimes, the tables are not in a suitable format for joining. For example, having multiple zones per country makes the CNTRY_NAME field unsuitable for aggregation. In such cases, pivoting the table is useful. This involves aggregating the fields for the zones and their respective area sizes under the country. The values in the ZONE column will be transformed into columns containing the area values. This way, you can aggregate this table with additional information that includes data on countries.

trans-unit.source: Selecting appropriate locations according to a set of criteria
trans-unit.target: Selecting appropriate locations according to a set of criteria

trans-unit.source: Interpolation
trans-unit.target: Interpolation

trans-unit.source: Spatial interpolation uses point data to estimate values at other unknown points. This is extremely useful for spatial phenomena that are [continuous](link), such as rainfall or temperature. For example, you have point data of the temperatures at weather stations, but you want to estimate the temperatures in between these points. Spatial Interpolation can estimate the temperature in between those points. This form of interpolation is called a __statistical surface__. Interpolation can be used to calculate missing elevation data, precipitation, snow accumulation, water table, and population density.
trans-unit.target: Spatial interpolation uses point data to estimate values at other unknown points. This is extremely useful for spatial phenomena that are [continuous](link), such as rainfall or temperature. For example, you have point data of the temperatures at weather stations, but you want to estimate the temperatures in between these points. Spatial Interpolation can estimate the temperature in between those points. This form of interpolation is called a __statistical surface__. Interpolation can be used to calculate missing elevation data, precipitation, snow accumulation, water table, and population density.

trans-unit.source: Interpolating data can be highly useful since an extensive data collection is costly and rarely possible. Data collection for continuous phenomena is usually conducted only at a small number of locations. Interpolation models use these points to calculate a raster surface with estimated values for each raster cell.
trans-unit.target: Interpolating data can be highly useful since an extensive data collection is costly and rarely possible. Data collection for continuous phenomena is usually conducted only at a small number of locations. Interpolation models use these points to calculate a raster surface with estimated values for each raster cell.

trans-unit.source: There are many different interpolation methods, each suited for another type of phenomenon or able to take into account different characteristics. In GIS, the most commonly used interpolation methods are __Spline interpolation__, __Inverse Distance Weighted Interpolation (IDW)__, and __Kriging__. In the following subchapters, we will take a look at these methods, and discuss their strengths and shortcomings.
trans-unit.target: There are many different interpolation methods, each suited for another type of phenomenon or able to take into account different characteristics. In GIS, the most commonly used interpolation methods are __Spline interpolation__, __Inverse Distance Weighted Interpolation (IDW)__, and __Kriging__. In the following subchapters, we will take a look at these methods, and discuss their strengths and shortcomings.

trans-unit.source: IDW Interpolation (Inverse Distance Weighted)
trans-unit.target: IDW Interpolation (Inverse Distance Weighted)

trans-unit.source: In the IDW interpolation method, the distance between a sample point and the point to be calculated determines how much the sample point's value influences the unknown point's value. A weighting coefficient is assigned to sample points, dictating how their influence decreases with increasing distance. The farther away a known sample point is, the less it influences the point being calculated.
trans-unit.target: In the IDW interpolation method, the distance between a sample point and the point to be calculated determines how much the sample point's value influences the unknown point's value. A weighting coefficient is assigned to sample points, dictating how their influence decreases with increasing distance. The farther away a known sample point is, the less it influences the point being calculated.

trans-unit.source: Keep in mind that IDW interpolation has a few __disadvantages__. For example, the quality of the calculated statistical surface decreases, if the distribution of sample points is uneven. Additionally, the highest and lowest values in the interpolated surface only occur at sample points, which is probably not the case in the real world. This often results in peaks or pits around the sample data points (see [IDW interpolation example](/content/Module_6/en_qgis_data_analysis_theorie.md#Interpolation)) (adopted from the [QGIS documentation](https://docs.qgis.org/2.18/de/docs/gentle_gis_introduction/spatial_analysis_interpolation.html)).
trans-unit.target: Keep in mind that IDW interpolation has a few __disadvantages__. For example, the quality of the calculated statistical surface decreases, if the distribution of sample points is uneven. Additionally, the highest and lowest values in the interpolated surface only occur at sample points, which is probably not the case in the real world. This often results in peaks or pits around the sample data points (see [IDW interpolation example](/content/Module_6/en_qgis_data_analysis_theorie.md#Interpolation)) (adopted from the [QGIS documentation](https://docs.qgis.org/2.18/de/docs/gentle_gis_introduction/spatial_analysis_interpolation.html)).

trans-unit.source: Spline Interpolation
trans-unit.target: Spline Interpolation

trans-unit.source: Triangulated Irregular Network
trans-unit.target: Triangulated Irregular Network

trans-unit.source: TIN interpolation is commonly called __Delauny triangulation__. This interpolation methods creates a triangular surface with its nearest neighbour points. In order to achieve this, circles are added around known sample points and the intersection of these circles are used as corners of the triangle (see {numref}`TIN_interpolation_example`). TIN interpolation is usually used to compute __digital elevation models__ (DEM).
trans-unit.target: TIN interpolation is commonly called __Delauny triangulation__. This interpolation methods creates a triangular surface with its nearest neighbour points. In order to achieve this, circles are added around known sample points and the intersection of these circles are used as corners of the triangle (see {numref}`TIN_interpolation_example`). TIN interpolation is usually used to compute __digital elevation models__ (DEM).

trans-unit.source: The problem with TIN statistical surfaces is that the surfaces are not smooth and may seem jagged, since they are based on triangles of varying sizes. Furthermore, triangulation is no suited to extrapolate data beyond the area where sample points have been collected (adopted from the [QGIS documentation](https://docs.qgis.org/2.18/de/docs/gentle_gis_introduction/spatial_analysis_interpolation.html))
trans-unit.target: The problem with TIN statistical surfaces is that the surfaces are not smooth and may seem jagged, since they are based on triangles of varying sizes. Furthermore, triangulation is no suited to extrapolate data beyond the area where sample points have been collected (adopted from the [QGIS documentation](https://docs.qgis.org/2.18/de/docs/gentle_gis_introduction/spatial_analysis_interpolation.html))

trans-unit.source: Kriging
trans-unit.target: Kriging

trans-unit.source: Kriging is a method of geostatistics used to estimate values for spatial units where the phenomenon of interest has not been measured at every spatial points. Kriging integrates covariates into the interpolation. For example, it is not only the distance to measured temperatures that influences its weighting; temperature is also influenced by the altitude of the sample point.
trans-unit.target: Kriging is a method of geostatistics used to estimate values for spatial units where the phenomenon of interest has not been measured at every spatial points. Kriging integrates covariates into the interpolation. For example, it is not only the distance to measured temperatures that influences its weighting; temperature is also influenced by the altitude of the sample point.

trans-unit.source: Outlook
trans-unit.target: Outlook

trans-unit.source: There are many analysis methods in GIS. However, setting up an analysis method can be quite time consuming, and creating a new analysis method for every research question makes it hard to compare the results of different analyses. This is why __model building__ and __automation__ are used frequently when working with GIS data. A model can be seen as a analysis blueprint that only needs input data to perform a certain type of analysis. Since the parameters are the same and similar datasets are needed for the model to work properly, the results can be compared. If you are interested in model building and automation, check out [module 7](/content/Module_7/en_qgis_automation_theory.md).
trans-unit.target: There are many analysis methods in GIS. However, setting up an analysis method can be quite time consuming, and creating a new analysis method for every research question makes it hard to compare the results of different analyses. This is why __model building__ and __automation__ are used frequently when working with GIS data. A model can be seen as a analysis blueprint that only needs input data to perform a certain type of analysis. Since the parameters are the same and similar datasets are needed for the model to work properly, the results can be compared. If you are interested in model building and automation, check out [module 7](/content/Module_7/en_qgis_automation_theory.md).
